James Skripchuk
Playing in everyone's backyard since 1998

The Story of MUSC106 - Exposition

In the last weeks Spring semester of freshman year, I got a mass email sent out by the UDCS department.

[CS-BS-FRESHMAN-2173] Call for Applications to be the first UD Computational Reasoning Fellows

  • Work up to 10 hours per week during the Fall and Spring semesters with faculty for mentoring, support, and feedback as they develop and teach activities, modules, and lesson plans that address computational thinking in discipline-specific courses during the summer and fall semesters

  • Serve as a peer leader in the faculty member’s course by supporting students participating in computational thinking activities

At this point in college, I was still looking for research opperutities; or at least something to keep me busy and give me a little bit of purpose. (You can read me wax poetic about my Freshman year in a later post.) At that time I was working in UD:VIP doing high-performance computing and parallel programming (which was also not going too well) So I jumped at the chance and filled out the application.

A few weeks later I got back a response, and I was accepted! The next steps were to attend a couple of introductory meetings where the heads of the program would explain the whole initiative and it’s goals.

So What’s Computational Thinking?

In my line of thinking, it’s to prevent this.

Wikipedia defines it as (taken from Jeannette M. Wing):

Computational Thinking is a set of problem-solving methods that involve expressing problems and their solutions in ways that a computer could execute.

UD created a general rubric that splits CT into four main pillars:

  • Decomposition
    • Breaks a problem into its constituent subproblems
  • Data
    • Analyze (or create) a data set that facilitates discovery of patterns and relationships
  • Algorithms
    • Creates a series of ordered steps to solve a problem or achive a goal
  • Abstraction
    • Reduces complexity to create a general representation of a process or group of objects so it is not only appropriate for the immediate purpose or goal but can also be used in different contexts

When I attended Computational Thinking conference after I was accepted to the program, one gentlemen raised his hand and asked “How is Computational Thinking different from normal problem solving skills?” It was a valid question, since on the surface both look like a solid framework for approaching a problem.
